questions.question: A bucket of water is swung in a vertical circle of radius $0.90\text{ m}$. What is the minimum speed at the top to prevent the water from spilling out? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.explanation: $v_{min} = \sqrt{gr} = \sqrt{10 \times 0.90} = \sqrt{9} = 3.0\text{ m/s}$. Below this speed, gravity exceeds the required centripetal force and water spills.

questions.question: A $0.50\text{ kg}$ ball on a $2.0\text{ m}$ string swings in a vertical circle at a speed of $8.0\text{ m/s}$ at the bottom. What is the tension in the string? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
A $0.50\text{ kg}$ ball on a $2.0\text{ m}$ string moves at $6.0\text{ m/s}$ at the top of a vertical circle. What is the tension in the string? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.explanation: $T = mv^2/r - mg = (0.50 \times 36/2.0) - 5.0 = 9.0 - 5.0 = 4.0\text{ N}$. At the top, gravity helps provide $F_c$, reducing tension.

questions.question: A ball on a string swings in a vertical circle. At which point is the tension in the string greatest?
options: At the top of the circle
At the bottom of the circle
questions.explanation: At the bottom, tension must support the weight AND provide centripetal force ($T = mg + mv^2/r$). At the top, weight helps provide $F_c$, so tension is least.

questions.question: At the bottom of a vertical circle of radius $1.5\text{ m}$, a ball moves at $6.0\text{ m/s}$ and the tension is $34\text{ N}$. What is its mass? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
Object A swings in a vertical circle of radius $r$ with critical speed $v$ at the top. Object B swings in a vertical circle of radius $4r$. What is its critical speed at the top?
questions.explanation: $v_{min} = \sqrt{gr} \propto \sqrt{r}$. Quadrupling $r$ gives $\sqrt{4r} = 2\sqrt{r}$, so the critical speed doubles: $v_B = 2v$.

questions.question: A roller coaster car of mass $500\text{ kg}$ moves at $20\text{ m/s}$ at the top of a circular loop of radius $20\text{ m}$. What is the normal force at the top? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
The same roller coaster car ($500\text{ kg}$) moves at the bottom of the loop (radius $20\text{ m}$) with speed $28.3\text{ m/s}$. What is the normal force? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
In a vertical loop, at which point is the normal force from the track on the coaster car the smallest?
questions.explanation: At the top, gravity helps provide centripetal force ($mg + F_N = mv^2/r$), so the track pushes less. At the bottom, the track must also support the car's weight, so $F_N$ is greatest.

questions.question: A roller coaster car enters a circular loop of radius $10\text{ m}$. What minimum speed must it have at the top to stay on the track? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.explanation: $v_{min} = \sqrt{gr} = \sqrt{10 \times 10} = \sqrt{100} = 10\text{ m/s}$. Below this, the car would fall off the track.

questions.question: A $2.0\text{ kg}$ mass swings in a vertical circle of radius $1.0\text{ m}$ at a constant speed of $5.0\text{ m/s}$. What is the difference between the tension at the bottom and the tension at the top? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
A ball on a string swings in a vertical circle. The string goes slack at the top when:
options: The ball is at the bottom
questions.explanation: At the instant the string goes slack, $T = 0$. Gravity alone provides the centripetal force: $mg = mv^2/r$, so $v = \sqrt{gr}$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered vertical circular motion and tension analysis!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the difference between tension at the top ($T = mv^2/r - mg$) and bottom ($T = mv^2/r + mg$).
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on the critical speed $v_{min} = \sqrt{gr}$ and identifying where forces add or subtract.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: at the top, weight helps provide $F_c$; at the bottom, weight opposes $F_c$.
